turns.text: Hello Gloria, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. It feels… strange to say it out loud, I think. Most people around here just talk about strollers and color schemes, but that’s not where my head is at.

I’m twenty-one, and I’m terrified. I’m sitting here in Wayland, surrounded by these woods that feel like they’re closing in, and all I can think about is that I’m trying to invite a soul into a world I’m still learning how to navigate myself. I’ve been reading so much—maybe too much—about how trauma gets passed down. It’s like I’m terrified of my own biology, wondering if my nerves are already wired to pass on all the things I’ve spent my life trying to untangle.

I went to my doctor recently to talk about preparing, but the appointment lasted ten minutes. She looked at my blood pressure and said I’m “perfect.” On paper, I guess I am. I’m young, I’m healthy, no “complications.” But she didn't look at *me*. She didn’t see the way my hands shake when the room gets too quiet. I’m trying to be better than my history, to build a secure base I never had, but the clinical side of care makes me feel so invisible. I just want someone to listen to the fear behind the vitals.
Tell me about the time when you first started thinking seriously about having a baby — what was going on in your life, and what did that feel like?

It’s hard to pin down the exact moment, but I think it started when I finally realized that my life didn’t have to be a carbon copy of the chaos I grew up in. I was sitting on this very porch, watching the light hit the trees, and for a split second, I didn't feel like I was just waiting for something bad to happen. That feeling was so foreign, so quiet, that I thought, *Maybe I could share this space with someone who’s never known the noise.*

But then the doubt rushes in like a tide. It’s like, who am I to talk about a "secure base" when I still flinch if a door closes too hard? I think I started wanting a baby because I want to prove that I can be the person who breaks the cycle. I want to nurture someone in a way that feels like healing for both of us. But honestly? It feels like standing on the edge of a cliff. I’m mourning the childhood I lost while desperately trying to architect a better one for a person who doesn't even exist yet. It’s this massive, heavy hope, but it’s tangled up in so much fear that I can’t tell which one is pulling harder.
What felt most important to you at that point — what were you hoping for or working towards?

Honestly, what I wanted more than anything was to be *seen*. Not as a "low-risk patient" or a young woman with a certain demographic profile, but as a person with a history that doesn’t just evaporate because my vitals are normal.

I’m working towards healing, but I’m realizing that the medical system feels like it’s built for bodies, not for the messy, complicated realities of how we carry our pasts. I’m searching for a provider who understands that a healthy pregnancy isn’t just about folic acid and blood pressure—it’s about preparing the soil, the emotional ground. I want someone who won’t look at me like I’m a problem to be solved or a box to be checked, but someone who sits with me when I talk about my fear of repeating the past.

I’m working towards being able to trust myself. Every day I’m here in this quiet house, I’m trying to practice being calm, to regulate my own nervous system so that when I *do* get pregnant, I’m not just a vessel for anxiety. I want to offer a child a version of me that is steady. But it’s hard to build that foundation when the people who are supposed to guide you through this process act like the inside of your head doesn't matter.
Tell me about how you went about preparing — or thinking about preparing — for pregnancy. What steps, if any, did you take, and what guided those choices?

I started by trying to "research" my way out of the fear. I read everything I could get my hands on—developmental psychology, studies on maternal-infant attachment, neurobiology. I thought if I understood the mechanics of how a brain develops, I could "outsmart" my own history. I wanted to map out a blueprint so that I wouldn't replicate the disorganized attachment I grew up with. It was my way of taking control, I guess. If I couldn't change my past, I could at least audit my own instincts.

I also tried to stabilize my environment. I moved here to Wayland because I thought the distance from the city, the silence, would help me regulate. But the isolation is a double-edged sword. When the thoughts get loud, there’s no one to break the echo.

I tried to bridge this into my healthcare. I made that appointment, thinking, *this is the first step.* I wanted to be honest about my trauma, about the mental health struggles that still surface, hoping for some kind of partnership, or at least a plan that acknowledged my history. Instead, the focus was entirely on my physical metrics. It felt like I was being prepared for a standard, factory-line experience, and that made me pull back. I’m still taking my supplements, and I’m trying to keep my body strong, but I feel like I’m doing it in a vacuum, without any real guidance on how to hold the weight of all this emotional preparation.
Where did you go to find out what you needed to know — friends, family, online, a doctor?

I didn't have anywhere—or anyone—else to go. My family back home is... well, they aren't the kind of people you turn to when you’re worried about intergenerational trauma or nervous system regulation. If I told them I was afraid of repeating the past, they’d tell me I’m overthinking things, that I should just be happy, or they’d make it about themselves. So, I keep it to myself. 

The internet has been my primary source, which is a dangerous place to be when you’re already spiraling. I spend hours reading journals and forums, trying to piece together a map for motherhood that I wasn't given. It’s like trying to learn a foreign language from a textbook while standing in the middle of a thunderstorm. I’ve looked for support groups, but they often feel too clinical or too "perfect-mom" focused. 

When I finally went to the doctor, I thought *that* was where the expert advice would come in. I thought there would be a nurse practitioner or a midwife who would ask, "Gloria, how are you actually doing?" Instead, the room felt sterile and rushed. I realized then that the information I really need—how to keep myself whole while growing a life—isn't in their brochures. It’s a lonely place to be, searching for a path that feels like it’s being erased as soon as you find it.
Tell me about any conversations you had with a GP, midwife, or other health professional before you became pregnant. What was that like?

It was deeply disappointing, honestly. I went in there with my heart racing, having prepared a mental list of things to say. I felt like I was handing over pieces of my life—my struggles with anxiety, the history of how things were in my house growing up—like I was showing them the cracks in my foundation so they could help me reinforce them before I added the weight of a baby. 

But it was like she didn’t hear a word. She just smiled, looked at her computer screen, and told me, "You’re young and healthy, Gloria. Don't worry, just start taking your vitamins and you’ll be fine."

"Fine" felt like an insult. I didn't want to be *fine*; I wanted to be ready. I wanted someone to acknowledge that I have a mountain to climb that other people might not see. I wanted a conversation about what support looks like when you don't have a village, or how to handle the inevitable triggers that come with a crying infant when your own nervous system is wired to panic. Instead, I left feeling like I was being dismissed as "low-risk." It made me feel like if I ever *do* struggle, I won't have a place to go, because on paper, I’m supposed to be doing just great.
Tell me about what it was like to try to conceive — however long or short that period was for you. What stands out most from that time?

Even though I'm still technically in that phase, it’s like living in a constant state of "what if." Every day is a negotiation between the part of me that wants this more than anything and the part that is terrified of being a mother who is just as broken as the one who raised me. 

What stands out most is the silence. You’d think it would be a time for bonding or talking about the future, but for me, it’s a time of intense, quiet surveillance. I spend my days hyper-analyzing myself. Am I calm enough? If I feel a flash of irritation because a neighbor’s car backfired, I immediately think, *There. That’s the cortisol. That’s what the baby will feel.* It’s like I’m constantly taking my own emotional temperature, terrified that I’m not stable enough to host another life.

It feels lonely, even when I’m just walking to the grocery store. I see other women—sometimes they look so confident, so "normal"—and I wonder if they’re also performing this internal audit, or if they’re just... okay. I’m holding my breath waiting for a positive test, but I’m also holding my breath because I’m afraid of what that little life will see when they finally look up at me.
Tell me about the picture you had in your mind of what pregnancy and becoming a parent would be like — where did those ideas come from?

My ideas mostly come from the things I *didn't* have. When I close my eyes and try to picture it, I see a quiet, sunlit room. I imagine a version of myself who, when the baby cries, doesn't immediately feel that familiar, sharp jolt of panic in my chest. Instead, I picture myself breathing into the space between the sound and my reaction, offering a steady, rhythmic touch. I imagine a home where a door slamming is just a sound, not a siren.

I think a lot of these images come from the books I’ve read on attachment—the "ideal" scenes of co-regulation that I never got to experience. I’m essentially trying to write a script for a play I’ve never seen performed. It’s an act of defiance, really. I’m trying to create a memory for my child that I don’t have in my own history.

But there’s a darker, more intrusive image, too. Sometimes, I picture myself freezing. I worry that when the reality of sleeplessness and the pressure of it all hits, I’ll slip into those old, disorganized patterns of my childhood. I’m terrified that the "secure base" I’m trying to build will reveal itself to be made of sand because the foundation underneath—my own nervous system—still feels so shaky. I’m building a house, but I’m constantly checking for cracks in the floorboards.
How much were those ideas shaped by your own family's experiences — your mother's, or others close to you?

My mother is the ghost in every room I walk into. Everything I’m planning—the way I want to hold the baby, the way I want to speak, the patience I’m trying to cultivate—is a direct reaction to her. Growing up, love felt like a transaction, or worse, like a sudden storm you had to hide from. If I did something right, there was praise, but if I faltered, there was this cold, unpredictable distance. I never knew which version of her I was going to get, and that’s why I have this disorganized attachment. It’s like my internal compass is constantly spinning because I never had a "True North" to rely on.

I see her everywhere. Sometimes I catch my reflection in the window and I see her eyes, or I hear my own voice get sharp over something small, and I freeze. I immediately think, *Oh god, am I becoming her?* It’s exhausting. It’s like I’m constantly deconstructing her methods just so I can do the exact opposite. 

I don't have other examples, really. My aunts are like her, and my friends are at a different stage or living lives that look so much more... cushioned. So, it’s just me, fighting a shadow. My preparation isn't just about nutrition or vitamins; it’s an exorcism, trying to clear the space so I can finally just be a mother, not a victim of my own history.
Tell me about any financial, practical, or life circumstances that were part of the picture as you were thinking about having a baby.

The financial part is like a weight pressing down on my chest. I’m working full-time, but it’s the kind of work that doesn’t leave much room for "extra." Wayland is expensive, and just keeping this small apartment means I’m always balancing on a knife’s edge. Every time I look at a price tag for baby clothes or think about the cost of prenatal care, my stomach turns. I wonder how I’m supposed to provide a sense of security—which is what a child needs most—when I’m constantly worrying about how to keep the lights on.

Then there’s the isolation. I don't have a partner, and my family is miles away, which is probably for the best, but it leaves a massive hole where a support system should be. If I’m sick, if I’m exhausted, or if the trauma hits me on a Tuesday afternoon and I just can’t function, there’s no one to tag in. It’s just me. I feel like I’m trying to solve a puzzle where the pieces are missing. I’m thinking about things like childcare and maternity leave, and the reality is that I’m one bad week away from everything unraveling. I want to bring a child into this, but I feel like I’m constantly calculating if I’m enough—if *my life* is enough—to hold someone else.
Were there things you felt you needed to have 'in place' before starting, and did that match what actually happened?

I had this very rigid list in my head. I thought I needed a certain amount of money in my savings account, a "stable" job with benefits, and a calm, perfectly ordered apartment. I thought if I could just organize the external world well enough, my internal world would finally quiet down. I told myself, *“Once I have this much saved, or once I find that one provider who actually listens, I’ll be ready.”*

But the reality hasn't matched that at all. The money never feels like enough, the apartment still feels like a hollow shell, and I certainly haven’t found that provider. I’m still standing here at the starting line, waiting for the "right" time, only to realize that the "right" time is probably a myth. I’m starting to see that I was looking for external permission—for a doctor to tell me I was okay, for my bank account to hit a certain number—before I would allow myself to be a mother. 

I’ve realized that no amount of planning can insulate me from the vulnerability of this. The "in place" stuff is all just armor, and deep down, I know that armor won't protect me from the emotional work of the journey. I’m still waiting, still preparing, but the goalposts keep moving. It’s frustrating. I’m trying to control the uncontrollable.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I guess the only thing I’d add is that I really hope people who work in maternity care understand that the silence in a room can be just as loud as the words spoken. 

When a doctor ignores the emotional context of a patient's life, they aren't just being "efficient"—they’re inadvertently reinforcing the idea that the internal experience doesn't count. For women like me, who are trying to break cycles and heal old wounds, that silence can be silencing. It makes us feel like we’re just a vessel for a future person, rather than a whole human being who needs to be supported, too.

I want to be more than just a successful pregnancy outcome on a chart. I want to be seen as someone doing the hardest work of my life—trying to unlearn a past I didn't choose so I can offer a future I’m choosing for someone else. If the medical system could just carve out five minutes to look past the blood pressure and the age, to actually look at the person sitting in front of them with all her trembling and her hope... that would change everything. Thank you for listening to me. Truly. It’s been a long time since I felt like I was being heard.
